title: Savoury pithivier
subtitle: A puff pastry dome filled with mushroom, spinach and cheese
excerpt: A savoury pithivier is two circles of puff pastry sealed around a filling, then scored with curved lines and baked until the pastry is deep brown and risen. The mushroom and spinach filling is seasoned with garlic and thyme and held together with cream cheese. It is served warm in wedges.
sourceNotes: The pithivier takes its name from the town of Pithiviers in the Loiret department of France, historically known for its almond cream-filled pastry. The round dome shape with curved scoring lines is consistent across both sweet and savoury applications. Savoury pithiviers using vegetable and cheese fillings are documented in modern French and British baking books. This recipe is synthesised from the established tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate up to 4 hours ahead, covered loosely. Egg-wash and score just before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan or conventional; a high temperature drives up the puff
baking.decoratingTechnique: curved score lines, egg wash glaze

glossaryTerms.definition: The curved lines on top of a pithivier are made with the tip of a sharp knife, drawing arcs from the centre peak to the edge without cutting all the way through the pastry. Six to eight evenly spaced arcs is the traditional look. The scoring is done after egg-washing and just before baking. It allows steam to escape during baking and gives the cooked pastry its distinctive windmill appearance.

content.text: The filling must be completely dry before it goes into the pastry. Wet filling will turn the bottom layer of puff pastry soggy and prevent it puffing. Cook the mushrooms over high heat until all their liquid has evaporated, then spread on a tray to cool before using. The cream cheese holds the filling in a cohesive layer when sliced.
is done with the tip of a sharp knife, not a bread knife. Draw arcs from the top, pressing lightly; cutting through the pastry will let the filling escape.

Melt the 20 g butter in a large pan over high heat. Add the 350 g finely chopped mushrooms and cook, stirring, for 8 to 10 minutes until all the moisture has evaporated and the mushrooms are dry and starting to colour. Add the garlic and thyme leaves and cook for 1 minute more. Add the 150 g spinach and stir until wilted, about 2 minutes. Season with salt. Spread on a tray and leave to cool completely, at least 20 minutes. Once cold, stir in the 100 g cream cheese and 50 g grated gruyere. The mixture should hold together when scooped.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Cut two 24 cm circles from the puff pastry. Place one circle on the tray. Mound the filling in the centre, leaving a 3 cm border all around; flatten it into a round dome. Brush the border with beaten egg. Lay the second pastry circle over the filling, pressing the edges firmly to seal. Crimp the edges with a fork or pinch. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C. Brush the top of the chilled pithivier all over with beaten egg. Using the tip of a sharp knife, score curved lines from the top of the dome to the edges in a windmill pattern, pressing lightly without cutting through the pastry. Make a small hole in the centre to let steam escape. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is deep brown and well puffed. Leave to rest for 10 minutes before cutting into wedges.

items.solution: The filling was too wet. Cook the mushrooms until completely dry before adding the spinach, and cool the filling fully before assembling. Any residual moisture will steam the base rather than bake it.

The edges were not sealed firmly enough, or they were cut too close to the filling. Press the border firmly all the way around and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before baking; chilling the butter in the pastry makes it puff upward rather than outward.
